preFix.title: Fitting a security door chain
preFix.subtitle: Fix a door chain or bar to allow partial opening while keeping the door secured
preFix.excerpt: How to fit a door chain or door bar to a front door. Covers positioning the chain plate and wall plate, the correct fixings for a timber door frame and a masonry wall, and why the position relative to the door hinges matters.
preFix.sourceNotes: Traditional UK domestic security hardware fitting practice.

content.text: A door chain is only as strong as its fixings. A chain held by 20 mm screws into a soft pine door frame resists a single firm push; a chain fixed with 50 mm screws into hardwood or masonry resists sustained force. Fit the chain correctly or it provides false reassurance rather than real security.
Mount the chain between 1400 mm and 1700 mm from the floor. This puts it within reach of adults and above the reach of young children. Fit both plates at the same height so the
works correctly when the door is at its furthest chained position.
Step 2, Mark and fix the door plate

Hold the door plate (the one with the channel the chain slides in) against the door face at the chosen height, approximately 50 mm from the door edge on the lock side. Mark the screw holes. Pre-drill with a 2 mm bit to prevent the door rail splitting. Drive the screws using the longest screws supplied, or upgrade to No. 8 × 38 mm screws if the supplied screws are shorter. They must reach the main door rail, not just the thin facing panel.
Step 3, Mark and fix the frame plate

Close the door partially, slip the chain into the door plate, and mark where the chain end reaches on the door frame. This marks the strike plate position, the chain end must be able to sit in the strike without being slack (which allows too much door movement) or taut (which makes it hard to engage). Fix the frame plate at this position using 50 mm screws driven into the solid timber door frame, not into the architrave.

With the chain engaged, the door should open by approximately 70 to 100 mm and be held firmly. The chain should not be so tight that it is impossible to slip in, nor so loose that the door swings well open. Apply the chain from inside and pull the door firmly to test the fixing resistance. It should not flex at the screws.

Door bar (security bar): a rigid steel bar fits into a strike plate on the door frame and a bracket on the door face. More resistant to forced entry than a chain because the bar is solid. Fitting procedure is the same.
UPVC door: UPVC doors have a reinforced steel core in the door frame. Use machine screws into the steel frame where possible, or specialist UPVC fixings from the door hardware supplier.

items.fix: Move the frame plate closer to the door hinge side, or fit a longer chain.
items.cause: Frame plate fitted too far from the door edge, or chain too short
items.symptom: Chain does not engage, the chain end cannot reach the frame plate

items.fix: Re-drill with longer screws driven into the solid timber frame behind the architrave.
items.cause: Screws too short, or fixed into the thin architrave rather than the solid frame
items.symptom: Screws pull out under a sharp tug

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
